sources.notes: Read the full easy-version statement and constraints: n<=400, m<=min(400,n(n-1)/2), sums of n and m <=400, sum q<=3*10^5.

Official E1 implementation sorts all m edges, starts from unweighted all-pairs distances, applies an O(n^2) APSP relaxation for every edge changed from cost 1 to 0, stores every state, and binary-searches queries.

Inspected accepted E1 code: groups equal weights, applies endpoint Floyd relaxations for every edge in the group, stores one APSP matrix per distinct threshold, and binary-searches the first distance <=k-1.
Independent E1/E2 explanation cross-checking the threshold-to-0/1-shortest-path equivalence and O(m n^2) easy preprocessing.
Independent writeup confirms f[threshold][a][b], monotone binary search, and the transition from E1's all-edge sweep to E2's DSU compression.
Independently proved the threshold equivalence and one-edge APSP recurrence; brute-checked kth-largest path answers and incremental matrices on 480 random connected graphs.

reference_candidates.notes: Additional Luogu solution index; not used because the consulted editorial, accepted code, and independent blogs were sufficient.

annotation.summary: Easy version: for each (a,b,k), minimize the kth-largest edge weight over all a-b paths; the sparse bound is m<=400 and total m<=400.
annotation.constraints: n<=400, m<=min(400,n(n-1)/2), q<=3*10^5; sums of n and m are <=400 and sum q<=3*10^5. This permits O(m n^2) preprocessing and O(log m) queries.
annotation.core_idea: For a candidate threshold X, give each edge cost 0 if w<=X and 1 otherwise. A path's new cost is exactly its number of edges heavier than X, so its kth-largest edge is <=X iff that cost is at most k-1. Therefore the query answer is the smallest X with d_X(a,b)<k. Initially all edges cost 1, so compute unweighted APSP by Floyd-Warshall. Sort edges by weight and turn them to cost 0. After inserting zero edge (u,v), any new shortest path uses it at most once, hence d'(i,j)=min(d(i,j),d(i,u)+d(v,j),d(i,v)+d(u,j)) in O(n^2). Store a matrix after each edge or equal-weight group. Distances are nonincreasing with X, so binary-search the first stored state with distance <k.
annotation.complexity: O(n^3+m n^2+m log m+q log m) time and O((m+1)n^2+m) memory.
tricks: Convert kth-largest-on-a-path into counting edges above a threshold.
Represent the thresholded graph with 0/1 edge costs.
Update all-pairs distances in O(n^2) after one edge changes from 1 to 0.
Exploit monotonicity in the sorted threshold states for every query.
The easy-specific m<=400 bound allows retaining all m APSP snapshots.

solution_variants.name: easy-all-edge-apsp-snapshots
solution_variants.summary: Easy-only preprocessing: sort all edges, perform an O(n^2) incremental Floyd relaxation for every edge (or weight group), retain all threshold distance matrices, and binary-search each query's first feasible threshold.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^3+m n^2+m log m+q log m) time, O((m+1)n^2) memory.

tags.evidence: Each query is reduced to the all-pairs shortest number of above-threshold edges in a 0/1 weighted graph.
The solution computes initial APSP with Floyd-Warshall and uses its O(n^2) one-edge insertion recurrence for every threshold state.
tags.description: Use Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest paths, including Floyd-style O(n^2) relaxations after inserting one nonnegative edge, as the central engine on a small graph.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/shortest-path tag is too broad to retrieve small-n APSP and incremental-Floyd problems such as both CF2057E versions. Main-agent taxonomy review is required before creating this candidate.

tags.evidence: As X increases, more edges become zero and every d_X(a,b) is nonincreasing, making the first state with d_X<k well-defined and searchable.
Each of up to 3*10^5 queries binary-searches the monotone sequence of stored threshold distances.
Edges are processed in nondecreasing weight so each stored state corresponds to a candidate answer threshold.
All threshold states are precomputed once from sorted edges before the large query batch is answered.
